svg.text: ANCIENT EGYPTIAN
SCRIPT — HIEROGLYPHS AND THE ROSETTA STONE
HIEROGLYPHIC WRITING SYSTEM
~700 standard hieroglyphs; logograms + phonograms combined
Three types: logogram (word) / phonogram (sound) / determinative (category)
Uniliteral signs (alphabet-like): 24 consonant signs exist
Hieratic (cursive) and Demotic (even more cursive): later developments
DIRECTION AND ORIENTATION
Can be read right-to-left, left-to-right, or top-to-bottom
Direction: follow the gaze of animal/human signs (they face the start)
Columns: typically read right-to-left (in most monumental texts)
THE THREE SCRIPTS OF THE ROSETTA STONE
Rosetta Stone (196 BCE): decree of Ptolemy V in three scripts
Top: hieroglyphic (sacred script for monuments)
Middle: Demotic (everyday Egyptian script of the era)
Bottom: Greek (language of the Ptolemaic government)
CHAMPOLLION'S DECIPHERMENT (1822)
Champollion: used Greek text to decode royal cartouches in hieroglyphs
Ptolemy and Cleopatra names in Greek matched cartouche letters
GREEK · SECONDARY LANGUAGE
GREEK ALPHABET: ROSETTA KEY
Greek text on Rosetta Stone: the only fully readable section in 1799
Greek alphabet: 24 letters, fully voweled, left-to-right
Classical Greek: upper/lowercase developed later; all caps in ancient texts
COPTIC: THE BRIDGE BETWEEN THEM
Coptic = Ancient Egyptian written in Greek alphabet + 6 Egyptian letters
Champollion used Coptic to understand Egyptian vowels (hieroglyphs: no vowels)
Coptic = critical missing link; Greek letters unlocked Egyptian sounds
SCRIPT COMPARISON
Egyptian: logographic + phonographic; complex; no vowels written
Greek: pure alphabet; simple; all vowels written
The Rosetta Stone: these two scripts on one stone unlocked history
Without Greek, hieroglyphs would remain unreadable for far longer
PLATE 61a ALT · ANCIENT EGYPTIAN / GREEK · SCRIPT
